coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescence assay for the detection of glyphosate with NACCu2+ complex
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiuhuan
Li, Shijie
Wang, Yujiong
Yang, Liu
Yue, Mingli
Liu, Yulong
Ye, Fei
Fu, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Glyphosate is widely used as an herbicide in weed control. However, the excessive use and residue of glyphosate adversely affect the environment. Thus, a rapid and highly sensitive system must be developed for glyphosate detection. Herein, a novel turn-on fluorescent probe was designed and synthesized for glyphosate, that is N-butyl-1,8-naphthalimide-4-hydrazino-6-isopropyl-chromone (NAC). The fluorescence of NAC was quenched by the addition of Cu2+ to form NACCu2+ complex in dimethyl sulfoxide/2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl]ethanesulfonic acid (DMSO/HEPES, 9/1, v/v, pH = 7.0). Upon the addition of glyphosate, the fluorescence of NACCu2+ was recovered through chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate. The NACCu2+ complex exhibited the desired linearity of glyphosate concentration under optimum conditions in the range of 0–40 μM with a low detection limit of 36 nM. Based on competitive coordination, NACCu2+ exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity for glyphosate. Moreover, NAC was successfully utilized to detect glyphosate in tap water, local water from Songhua River, soil, maize, and soybean with convenient operations, indicating a promising application in pesticide residue detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Probe
NAC–Cu2+
Glyphosate
Off–on
Competitive coordination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of universal solution of various ions and pesticides
Application of glyphosate in real samples
Fluorescence response of NAC probe to Cu2+
Fluorescence assay of glyphosate by NACCu2+
Sensing mechanism for glyphosate recognition by NACCu2+
Detection of glyphosate in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51903032 ), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (No. LBH-Z19045 ), and “Young Talents” Project of Northeast Agricultural University (No. 18QC64 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis (a and b) and fluorescence spectral (c and d) changes of NAC toward various ions.
(a) Fluorescence spectra of NAC with different amounts of Cu2+ added; (b) linear relationship of NAC fluorescence intensity with Cu2+ concentration.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum change of NACCu2+ solution after the addition of different pesticides (1: glufosinate, 2: trichlorfon, 3: phosethy-Al, 4: oxyfluorfen, 5: fosthiazate; 6: mesotrione, 7: teflubenzuron; 8: pyrazoxyfen; 9: isoxaflutole; 10: diflubenzuron; 11: flusilazole; 12: fluazuron; 13: flumioxazin; 14: triflumuron; Gly: glyphosate); (b) fluorescence intensity of NACCu2+ in the presence of various competing pesticides.

Confocal fluorescence microscopic images of 293 T cell lines under different conditions: (a, c, and k) Blank cell, (b, g, and l) NAC, (c, h, and m), NACCu2+ (10 μM), (d, i, and n) NAC–Cu2+–glyphosate (10 μM); (e, j, and o) NAC–Cu2+–glyphosate (20 μM).
Synthetic approach to the NAC chemosensor.
Possible mechanism of NAC and Cu2+.
Possible sensing mechanism of NACCu2+ and glyphosate.
Application of NACCu2+ to glyphosate in real samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescence assay for the detection of glyphosate with NACCu2+ complex

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Arts and Sciences, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Arts and Sciences, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Arts and Sciences, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin 150030, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glyphosate is widely used as an herbicide in weed control. However, the excessive use and residue of glyphosate adversely affect the environment. Thus, a rapid and highly sensitive system must be developed for glyphosate detection. Herein, a novel turn-on fluorescent probe was designed and synthesized for glyphosate, that is N-butyl-1,8-naphthalimide-4-hydrazino-6-isopropyl-chromone (NAC). The fluorescence of NAC was quenched by the addition of Cu2+ to form NACCu2+ complex in dimethyl sulfoxide/2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazin-1-yl]ethanesulfonic acid (DMSO/HEPES, 9/1, v/v, pH = 7.0). Upon the addition of glyphosate, the fluorescence of NACCu2+ was recovered through chelation between Cu2+ and glyphosate. The NACCu2+ complex exhibited the desired linearity of glyphosate concentration under optimum conditions in the range of 0–40 μM with a low detection limit of 36 nM. Based on competitive coordination, NACCu2+ exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity for glyphosate. Moreover, NAC was successfully utilized to detect glyphosate in tap water, local water from Songhua River, soil, maize, and soybean with convenient operations, indicating a promising application in pesticide residue detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All of the data is presented in the article and associated Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (N-(phosphonomethyl) glycine) is an organic phosphine herbicide, has the characteristics of high efficiency, broad spectrum, low toxicity, and safety, and is a cost effective pesticide variety. Given its excellent herbicidal activity, glyphosate has been widely used in weed control (Guo et al., 2014; Jiménez-López et al., 2020; Rawat et al., 2016). However, glyphosate easily causes the accumulation of residues in agricultural products and drinking water, posing a serious threat to human health and the ecological environment (Tu et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2019b; del Carmen Aguirre et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2020). The maximum contaminant level for glyphosate in drinking water established by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency is 4.1 μM (Tu et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2019a; del Carmen Aguirre et al., 2019; Ren et al., 2020). Consequently, the demand for simple and effective methods for monitoring glyphosate in drinking water, surface water, and crops has grown.

Common traditional methods for glyphosate detection include high-performance liquid chromatography (Khrolenko and Wieczorek, 2005; Sun et al., 2010), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (Saito et al., 2011), ion chromatography (Zhu et al., 1999), capillary electrophoresis (Orejuela and Silva, 2005), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (Wang et al., 2016a). However, these approaches typically require sophisticated or expensive instruments, are costly, time consuming, and involve complicated operations, which limit their practical application. Colorimetric or fluorometric methods are notably simpler to operate, cost effective, and have lower detection limits (He et al., 2014; He et al., 2022; Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2022a, 2022b; Li et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2022; Yin et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2018). Fluorescence detection methods are highly sensitive and selective (Bettazzi et al., 2018; Cao et al., 2019b; Zambrano-Intriago et al., 2021). It can occur through various mechanisms and is ideal for glyphosate analysis. The optical signal collected by fluorescence detection is directly derived from the luminescent material; hence, finding luminescent materials with high fluorescence quantum yield and near-infrared emission is critical for building a highly sensitive diagnostic platform for glyphosate. To date, a few elegant optical probes can successfully detect glyphosate (Dai et al., 2014) (Scheme S1). Fluorescence-based assay has drawn considerable research attention for its remarkable features of high selectivity, sensitivity, quick detection speed, simple experiment process, ordinary instrumentation, and easy in situ detection.

1,8-Naphthimide and coumarin are important fluorophores with the merits of good photostability, large stock shift, and high fluorescence quantum yield (Ye et al., 2019). Coumarin-based fluorophores have the parent structure of α-benzopyrone and are excellent candidate in the design of fluorescent probes. Toxicological studies have shown that long-term exposure to glyphosate impact on human health in certain manner, particularly in terms of reproductive development. Studies have shown that when pregnant women are exposed to glyphosate during pregnancy, their offspring have birth defects including anencephaly, microcephaly and skull deformities (Buchenauer et al., 2022; Eskenazi et al., 2023; Kogevinas, 2021). Glyphosate inhibits the proliferation of cancer cells and promotes its apoptosis, which has a carcinogenic risk to human beings. Therefore, the importance of fluorescence imaging of glyphosate in living cells for the study of modern cell biology cannot be ignored. Fluorophore splicing is an effective means to prolonging the conjugated system and causing the emission wavelength to red shift, which is beneficial to cell imaging. Fluorescence imaging of living cells has made it possible to observe the dynamics and interactions of specific biomolecules over time. Has promoted the practical application of living cell imaging, including the development of fluorescent proteins as markers of gene expression and specific markers. Inspired by the merits of fluorescence technology and as continuation of our previous work (Fu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2021a; Sun et al., 2021b; Zhao et al., 2023a), copper ion complexes have been widely used because of its sensitivity, low toxicity, and good membrane permeability. A coumarin derivative showed selective fluorescence quenching of Cu2+. Glyphosate formed a strong complex with amine and carboxylate to restore fluorescence. A compound fluorescence-sensing system was constructed to realize the quantitative analysis of glyphosate (Wang et al., 2020). N-Butyl-1,8-naphthalimide-4-hydrazino-6-isopropyl-chromone (NAC) was synthesized (Scheme 1 ). NACCu2+ was used for the selective and sensitive detection of glyphosate with “off–on” fluorescence.
All reagents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used without further purification to prepare solutions with deionized water (Table S1).
The instruments used in the experiment and the underlying purposes are listed in Table S2.

In accordance with reported literatures, N-n-butyl-4-bromo-1,8-naphthalimide and N-n-butyl-4-hydrazine hydrate-1,8-naphthalic anhydride were synthesized (Bhorge et al., 2014; Chang et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2018). The complete synthesis route of the designed NAC fluorescence probe was described (Scheme 2 ). A total of 0.7 g intermediate (2) and 0.6 g raw material 3 were added to 50 mL EtOH. By stirring and refluxing at 78 °C for 6 h, the mixture participated in the reaction and was then cooled to room temperature. After filtering and drying, the coarse product was finally purified through column chromatography with ethyl acetate and petroleum ether elution system to afford compound NAC as a red solid (yield: 69.5 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of universal solution of various ions and pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of the NAC fluorescence probe (10−4 M) was prepared in DMSO and diluted with DMSO/HEPES (9/1, v/v, pH = 7.0) to prepare an analytical solution. The metal salts used included NaCl, KCl, CuCl, AgNO3, CuCl2, MgCl2, NiCl2, ZnCl2, SnCl2, BaCl2, MnCl2, CaCl2, HgCl2, CoCl2, PbCl2, FeCl2, AlCl3, and CrCl3. The 10−2 M solutions of anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CN−, SCN−, NO2 −, NO3 −, ClO4 −, SO4 2−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, and Ac−) were prepared using tetrabutylammonium salts with ultrapure water. Various commercially available pesticides, including glufosinate, trichlorfon, phosethy-Al, oxyfluorfen, fosthiazate, glyphosate, mesotrione, teflubenzuron, pyrazoxyfen, isoxaflutole, diflubenzuron, flusilazole, fluazuron, flumioxazin and triflumuron, were prepared as 10−2 M aqueous solutions.

In the selectivity measurement, 10.0 mL NAC solution (1 × 10−5 M) was collected with a pipette, 50 μL (10−2 M) stock solution of different metal ions and anions were obtained with a pipette gun, and they were added to 10 mL volumetric flasks. The prepared solution was ultrasonicated for a few seconds before the spectral property test. In the competitive experiment, 50 μL Cu2+ (10−2 M) solution was added to the volumetric flask of 10 mL NAC solution (1 × 10−5 M). The resulting solution was completely mixed. The same amount of other different metal ions was added, and the spectral properties were recorded. For the concentration titration experiment, different amounts of Cu2+ solution were added to the volumetric flask containing 10 mL probe NAC stock solution (1 × 10−5 M). The resulting solution was thoroughly mixed, and the spectral properties were recorded after several seconds of ultrasound. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) solution (10−2 M) was prepared with ultrapure water as the solvent for the reversible experiment. All fluorescence assays were performed at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Local tap water, Songhua River water, soil on campus, and soybeans (purchased from local supermarkets) were selected for experimental needs. Standard addition method was adopted for actual sample detection. Three parallel measurements were performed for each group to calculate the recovery rate in the test results. The applicability of probe-target detection in real samples was evaluated.

Specific measurement of water from Songhua River: Water sample from Songhua River was collected in a clean volumetric bottle. Prior to the measurement, the water sample was filtered with a 0.22 μM membrane and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove all dispersed impurities (Wang et al., 2016c).

Specific measurement steps for soil, maize, and soybeans: Grind 5 g of soil or soybean sample, mix with 100 mL of water, sonicate for 15 min, filter and centrifuge (5000 rpm) to obtain the supernatant. (Qu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2016b).
After standing for 3–5 min, different concentrations of glyphosate (20, 30, and 40 μM) standard solutions were added to the NACCu2+ complex of the above samples.

293 T cells were incubated in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium with 10 % fetal bovine serum, 1 % streptomycin, and 1 % penicillin. The cultures were maintained in a humidified incubator at 37 °C in air with 5 % CO2. One day before the fluorescence imaging experiment, the cells were placed on a 35-mm-diameter cover glass bottom petri dish (NESTC). Thereafter, cells were cultured with NAC (20 μM) for 30 min. Then, the cells were flushed with phosphate buffered saline (pH = 7.2) buffer solution to dispose the residuum of NAC. The cells loaded with NAC were treated with Cu2+ (20 μM) for 30 min. After removing the medium solution, cell images were obtained under a fluorescence microscope. 293 T cells incubated with the NACCu2+ complex were further treated with 10 and 20 μM glyphosate, and cell imaging was performed.

Maize was purchased from a nearby supermarket, steeped in deionized water at 35 °C for 30 min, and then soaked for 30 min with 0.6 % (w/v) carbendazim. Carbendazim was rinsed off, and maize was cultivated in a petri dish containing filter paper wet with water. The dish was placed in a constant-temperature incubator (30 °C). When the root length of maize reached 3–4 cm, the plants were treated with three groups of methods. The first group included three types: one plant was treated with deionized water and the other with an solution of Cu2+ (100 μM) for 24 h, and the last one was treated with a solution of glyphosate (100 μM) for 24 h. The second group included two types: one was cultured in a NAC (100 μM) aqueous solution for 2 h, and the other was cultured in a NACCu2+ (100 μM) aqueous solution for 2 h. The third group included two types: one was cultured in a aqueous solution of glyphosate (100 μM) for 24 h and then cultured in a NACCu2+ (100 μM) for 2 h, and the second was cultured in a NACCu2+ (100 μM) for 2 h and cultured in a aqueous solution of glyphosate (100 μM) for 2 h. All plants were treated with pure water before imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NAC probe to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Four organic solvents (C2H5OH, CH3CN, DMSO, and dimethyl formamide) were selected to detect the effects of different solvents on spectral properties of NAC solution, and the changes in fluorescence spectra of NAC solution in different proportions with water were investigated. In DMSO, the fluorescence intensity of free NAC was the strongest (Fig. S5a), and the UV–vis absorption spectra showed the maximum absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. S5b). Based on fluorescence response mechanism, solubility, and toxicity, DMSO/HEPES (9/1, v/v) was finally selected as the solvent for spectral analysis (Fig. S6).

The selectivity and sensitivity of NAC (10−5 M) in DMSO/HEPES solution (9/1, v/v, pH = 7.0) were evaluated. The free NAC exhibited strong fluorescence at an emission wavelength of 550 nm. The excitation wavelength was 450 nm (Fig. S7). After the addition of Cu2+ for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased and was completely quenched (Fig. S8). Almost no evident change in the UV–vis spectra of NAC was observed in the presence of 5 equiv. of metal ions (K+, Cu+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Sn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Na+, Fe2+, Al3+, Cr3+, and Ag+) (Fig. 1a) and anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CN−, SCN−, NO2 −, NO3 −, ClO4 −, SO4 2−, HSO4 −, H2PO4 −, and Ac−), except for Cu2+ (Fig. 1b). When Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence intensity of NAC significantly decreased, the yellow NAC solution became colorless, and the fluorescence intensity of NAC with other metal ions and anions were unchanged (Fig. 1c and d), which demonstrated the specificity of NAC for Cu2+.

The interference of other cations on the recognition of Cu2+ was investigated. The fluorescence of NAC did not change in the presence of other metal ions but was evidently quenched after the addition of Cu2+ (5 equiv.) (Fig. S9). The results show that the presence of other ions did not interfere with the detection of Cu2+. Thus, the NAC detected Cu2+ with good resistance to interference and recognized Cu2+ to a certain extent.

To explore the reversibility of NAC, we added EDTA (5 equiv.) to NACCu2+, and we observed the enhanced fluorescence of NACCu2+ (Fig. S10). Further, reversibility experiment was performed, with addition of the same amount of EDTA to trigger the fluorescence of NACCu2+ and the same amount of Cu2+ to quench it again (Fig. S11a). After several cycles, the results show that the complexation effect of EDTA on Cu2+ was greater than that of NAC, and it was used as a reversible probe for the detection of Cu2+. The test results also exhibited good repeatability and reproducibility.

The system pH had a certain influence on the response of the fluorescent probe. Thus, the recognition capability of the probe NAC at different pH was tested . As shown in Fig. S11b, the response of probe NAC to Cu2+ was explored in the range of pH = 1.0–12.0. The pH environment = 4.0–9.0 was more suitable for the recognition of Cu2+, which indicated that the NAC probe can recognize Cu2+ in environmental analysis, including physiological conditions.

Under optimal conditions, a variety concentration of Cu2+ was added in NAC solution and the fluorescence spectra were subsequently recorded. When 40 μL Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence emission of NAC decreased significantly and reached a platform in 550 nm (Fig. 2a). According to the calibration plot, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear relationship (R 2 = 0.98) with 0–40 μM Cu2+ (Fig. 2b), which indicated that NAC can be used to quantitatively determine Cu2+. The limit of detection (LOD) of NAC for Cu2+ sensing was 1.66 μM according to LOD = 3σ/k (σ: standard deviation of the maximum fluorescence intensity of the same probe sample measured 10 times, k: slope of the linear equation in the spectral concentration titration experiment).

To determine the stoichiometry of NAC combined with Cu2+, we added different concentrations of Cu2+ to the NAC solution. The stoichiometric relationship between compound NAC and Cu2+ was determined using Job's plot analysis (Fig. S12). The value of fluorescence intensity reached a minimum when the molar fraction (X) of NAC was 0.5. The results show that the ratio of interaction between NAC and Cu2+ was 1:1 (Gui et al., 2017).

The reaction mechanism of NACCu2+ was analyzed via FT-IR, NMR and ESI–MS (Fig. S13, Fig. S14 and Fig. S15). With the increased amount of Cu2+, the proton peak of amino group changed remarkably and gradually weakened. These findings in the interaction with Cu2+ further confirmed that the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process was inhibited and led to fluorescence quenching by NAC. This result further confirmed that NH-involved complexes contributed to the reflective PET process and led to fluorescence quenching (Scheme 2).

To further investigate the reactive sites of NAC recognition toward Cu2+, we selected the following 3-formyl-6-isopropylchromone and naphthalimide compounds with a common molecular skeleton as experimental objects to compare with the detection effect of NAC. Fig. S16a shows the fluorescence histogram of different compounds toward Cu2+, and Fig. S16b presents the schematic of fluorescence changes. When Cu2+ was added to different derivative systems, the response effect of N-n-butyl-4-hydrazine hydrate-1,8 naphthalic anhydride was similar to that of NAC. In the 3-formyl-6-isopropylchromone and N-n-butyl-4-bromo-1,8-naphthalimide systems, when Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence change was negligible. After 3-formyl-6-isopropylchromone and naphthalimide splicing, the conjugate structure of NAC system increased with the shift of redshift emission wavelength, conducive to in vivo imaging. The results show that the –NH-containing naphthalimide compounds possessed specific fluorescence responses toward Cu2+, and the more –NH groups in the probe structure, the better the fluorescence effect on pesticides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of glyphosate by NACCu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the capability of NACCu2+ complexes to specifically recognize glyphosate, we conducted spectroscopic studies of 15 pesticides (glufosinate, trichlorfon, phosethy-Al, oxyfluorfen, fosthiazate, glyphosate, mesotrione, teflubenzuron, pyrazoxyfen, isoxaflutole, diflubenzuron, flusilazole, fluazuron, flumioxazin and triflumuron) using NACCu2+ (Scheme S2). When glyphosate was added to NACCu2+, the solution changed from colorless to yellow, and the fluorescence intensity was associated with recovery (Fig. 3a). Moreover, the other pesticides barely exerted any effects. In the UV–vis spectrum, the absorption intensity increased only when glyphosate was added (Fig. S17). Glyphosate contains phosphonyl and carboxyl, which exhibited good chelation with Cu2+. This complexation weakened the complexation between Cu2+ and NAC, which resulted in the evident fluorescence recovery of NAC. Therefore, NACCu2+ for the highly sensitive detection of glyphosate was developed. The fluorescence spectra of NACCu2+ were investigated to examine its selectivity in the existence of other pesticides. When 5 equiv. of background pesticides (10−2 M) and glyphosate (10−2 M) were simultaneously added to the NACCu2+ system, the fluorescence intensity was almost the same as that in the presence of glyphosate. The detection of glyphosate by NACCu2+ was not influenced by the selected pesticides (Fig. 3b). Therefore, the combined results clearly indicated that NACCu2+ exhibited remarkable a signaling behavior for glyphosate and can function as a highly selective and disturbance-free fluorescent probe for glyphosate.

Under optimal conditions, the NACCu2+ system was incubated in different concentrations of glyphosate (Fig. S18a). The fluorescence intensity of NACCu2+ was enhanced with the increase in glyphosate concentration. A good linear relationship was established between fluorescence intensity and glyphosate concentrations (0–50 μM), and the correlation coefficient R
2 was 0.98 (Fig. S18b). The calculated LOD was 36 nM for glyphosate. Table 1 summarizes glyphosate detection using different chemical sensors (Table S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for glyphosate recognition by NACCu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Based on previous studies, glyphosate formed stable complexes with metal ions. The mode of glyphosate binding to Cu2+ was confirmed by extended fine-structure X-ray absorption spectra (Gui et al., 2017). The Cu2+ located at the center of the Jen–Teller twisted octahedron and amine, carboxyl, and phosphonate groups of glyphosate bound to Cu2+, forming two five-membered chelating rings which were arranged in the equatorial plane (Sheals et al., 2001). Glyphosate was recognized by the NACCu2+ complex system because glyphosate coordinated with Cu2+ and competed for NAC in the system (Scheme 3 ). As shown in Fig. S19, NAC did not specifically recognize glyphosate. The result of glyphosate addition to the NACCu2+ complex system was similar to that of EDTA. Thus, glyphosate was specifically recognized by the NACCu2+ complex system based on competitive coordination principle.

In addition to the exploration of detection mechanism, the general structure of the target analyte was further studied. As shown in Fig. S20, the effects of glyphosate and glufosinate on the test results were discussed. The NACCu2+ complex system exhibited a strong specific recognition capability for glyphosate because the carboxyl and amino of glyphosate phosphate esters were comparatively near anywhere in space, conducive to complex formation with Cu2+. Although glyphosate and glufosinate are structurally similar, the three methylene molecules separated the amino and phosphonate in glufosinate, which resulted in the difficult spatial complexation with Cu2+. However, other resembling pesticides or compounds cannot coordinate with Cu2+ because of the absence of coordination groups. To prove the above conjecture, we selected five compounds as experimental subjects. To ensure the rigor of the experiment, we detected 2-aminobutyric acid, alanine, sarcosine, glycine, and (aminomethyl)phosphonic (Fig. S20b), which are compounds having structural characteristics similar to those of glyphosate or glufosinate. However, none of the five compounds can enhance the fluorescence. According to these test results, the key structures leading to the “off–on” fluorescence of NACCu2+ were the amino and carboxyl close to the phosphate ester in space.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practicality of NACCu2+ for the detection of glyphosate, we tested the tap water, local water from Songhua River, soil collected from Northeast Agricultural University campus (Harbin, China), and commercially available maize and soybeans with different amounts of glyphosate (20, 30, and 40 μL). The satisfactory recoveries ranged from 98.3 % to 120.0 %, and the accuracy measurement range of RSD was 0.9–2.5 % (Table 1). Compared with the reported method (De Miranda Colombo and Masini, 2011; Guo et al., 2016), the recovery rate was 83.0 %–112.0 %, and the RSD was 0.5 %–10.0 %, which proved that the established approach was accurate and reliable. These results indicated that the NACCu2+ system is very convenient for monitoring of glyphosate in the environment and has great advantages.

Given the innovation in mobile phones, the provision of approved embedded sensors offers significant advantages over conventional methods in terms of speed of testing, ease of use, low price and compact devices. Therefore, these devices have been employed as analytical tools for multiple purposes (Gomez-Nicola et al., 2014; Ozcan, 2014). To detect glyphosate on smart phones (Fahimi-Kashani and Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020; Mani et al., 2020; Manna et al., 2020), we downloaded a freely available portable red, blue, and green (RGB) color value application online on a smartphone and used the rear camera of the phone to monitor the changes in RGB values of glass tubes containing solutions before and after the addition of different amounts of glyphosate. In general, the RGB range of the model is represented by the integer values of each of the three RGB colors from 0 to 255. In the range of 0–50 μM, the glyphosate concentration and R/G value exhibited a good linear fitting relationship (Fig. S21), and the LOD value was 1.0 μM. The NACCu2+ is therefore a useful tool compared with expensive and complex instruments.

Easy and rapid detection of analytes is one of the most important factors to evaluate the application of probes. The colorimetric experiment of NACCu2+ was carried out with paper as the carrier to further study whether NACCu2+ can be made into a practical portable detection device. Test strips of the same size were placed in the NACCu2+ and glyphosate solutions of different concentrations for half an hour. The test papers were dried by natural wind, and the effect was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp. The color of the test paper gradually turned yellow with the increase in glyphosate concentration (Fig. S22), demonstrating that NACCu2+ can be applied to paper test and fluorescence detection of glyphosate.

As low cytotoxicity is a key requirement for live-cell imaging, the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) test was used to determine the cytotoxicities of NAC, NACCu2+. Although living cells were incubated with 10 μM NAC, NACCu2+ for 12 h, the cells still maintained high activity (Fig. S23). We evaluated the application of NAC plot for detecting Cu2+ in biological systems in a preliminary fluorescence imaging assay using 293 T cells (Fig. 4 ). Cells incubated with the NAC probe at a concentration of 20 μM showed a significant fluorescence signal at an excitation wavelength of 450 nm, and the probe retained a “fluorescence-on” morphology in live cells. In contrast, 293 T cells incubated with the NAC probe beforehand and treated separately with an additional 20 μM Cu2+ showed no significant fluorescence signal. These results indicated that NAC can specifically detect Cu2+ in vivo. Finally, the ability of NACCu2+ to detect glyphosate in 293 T cells was further investigated. Cells were incubated with NACCu2+ (10 μM) for 30 min at 37 °C. The addition of 10 μM glyphosate increased the fluorescence intensity. 293 T cells incubated with the NACCu2+ probe were further treated with 20 μM glyphosate, and a significant fluorescence signal was observed. Confocal microscopy studies evidence the good permeability and compatibility of the NACCu2+ probe for intracellular monitoring of glyphosate, indicating that NACCu2+ can be used as a sensor for glyphosate imaging in live cells.

Currently, pesticide residue detection has been extensively studied in cells images. However, plant tissue imaging is rarely reported (Kundu et al., 2019; Shang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2023b). Therefore, the biological imaging of maize in Cu2+ solution by NAC probe and NACCu2+ in glyphosate solution was studied. Further experiments were performed in Cu2+, glyphosate, and NAC solutions with freshly sprouted maize as the subject. The control plants showed no fluorescence (Figs. S24a–24c), and the plants treated with NAC solution exhibited a strong yellow fluorescence (Fig. S24d). The complexation of NAC with Cu2+ caused the fluorescence quenching of plants (Fig. S24e). The interaction between NACCu2+ and glyphosate led to fluorescence enhancement of plants (Figs. S24f–24 g). These results confirmed that NAC is cell-permeable and capable of imaging Cu2+ and glyphosate in plant tissues.

In conclusion, a turn-on NAC fluorescence probe was synthesized for glyphosate detection with NACCu2+ complex. The fluorescence emission of NAC was effectively quenched by Cu2+. In the presence of glyphosate, the system released free NAC through competitive coordination, which made the fluorescence intensity recover obviously. Additionally, the LOD of NACCu2+ for glyphosate was 36 nM. NAC exhibited good membrane permeability and low cytotoxicity in live cells as well as outstanding imaging capabilities for Cu2+ and glyphosate. In addition, NACCu2+ was applied to detect glyphosate in water, local soil, and commercially available maize and soybean, and the recovery rate was satisfactory. Imaging of plants demonstrated excellent imaging against Cu2+ and glyphosate. The probe can thus be applied in glyphosate residue detection in real samples.
Conceptualization/Methodology: Ying Fu conceived and designed the experiment and conveyed the manuscript information as corresponding authors.
Validation: Qiuhuan Liu and Shijie Li repeated the experiment multiple and then used it as an in-depth research content.
Formal analysis: Qiuhuan Liu and Shijie Li used Origin and Excel to process the data, Benesi-Hildebrand and LOD = 3σ/K a formula data calculation, shooting by mobile phone.
Investigation: Yujiong Wang, Liu Yang and Mingli Yue consulted related literature, collected background knowledge and theoretical support.
Data Curation: Yulong Liu coordinated all of the data.
Writing - Original/Review & Editing Draft: Qiuhuan Liu wrote the manuscript, edited by Ying Fu and Fei Ye.

The work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51903032), Postdoctoral Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (No. LBH-Z19045), and “Young Talents” Project of Northeast Agricultural University (No. 18QC64).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.163548.
